rationale: The citing paper uses Jap and Ganesan (2000) in a specific conceptual role, not just as a list citation: it is explicitly cited to support the claim that idiosyncratic investments can create receiver-side value and improve coordination. The paper also relies on that study as an empirical source for several scale items (e.g., buyer-specific investments and solidarity), indicating concrete measurement borrowing. In the discussion, Rokkan et al. position their time-horizon approach against prior relationship life-cycle work that includes Jap and Ganesan, so the citation helps frame both setup and contribution.
relation_description: Rokkan et al. treat Jap and Ganesan (2000) as prior grounding for how specific investments function in channel relationships and as a source for operationalizing key constructs. Their model then builds from that base by offering a different time-based framing (extendedness rather than life-cycle stages) for when expropriation versus bonding effects occur.
